# Scope, Ethics, and Safety Boundary

> **Use boundary:** This file is written for research, ethics, detection, and policy analysis. It describes mechanisms at a conceptual level so they can be studied, criticised, or detected. It is not an execution guide for manipulating platforms, audiences, investors, advertisers, or creators.


## In scope

- How platforms allocate attention through proxy signals.
- How those signals can be distorted.
- How regulators and platforms define prohibited behaviour.
- How researchers can detect signs of manufactured traction.
- How to distinguish legitimate growth from deceptive signal engineering.

## Out of scope

- Step-by-step instructions for manipulating platforms.
- Operational thresholds for evading detection.
- Tool setup for fake engagement, fake accounts, fraud, or deception.
- Scripts, automation flows, or specific procurement guidance.

## The ethical boundary

Legitimate growth improves truthful discovery. Manipulative growth falsifies the evidence of discovery.

| Allowed / defensible | Manipulative / deceptive |
|---|---|
| Better product, creative, offer, targeting | Fake engagement, fake reviews, fake accounts |
| Disclosed paid distribution | Hidden sponsorship or paid promotion disguised as organic |
| Real customer testimonials with context | Edited, fabricated, suppressed, or incentivized testimonials |
| Platform-compliant ads | Fraudulent traffic, invalid clicks, fake leads |
| Transparent creator partnerships | Creator farms or UGC camouflage without clear disclosure |
| Honest metric reporting | ARR inflation, GMV/revenue confusion, unsupported claims |

## Why this matters

Digital markets operate through shortcuts. Users rarely audit a product from scratch. They use proxies: ratings, reviews, views, comments, installs, launch rank, follower count, customer logos, app charts, and revenue metrics. The ethical problem arises when those proxies no longer reflect reality.
